results.output:
I'll review how `SubprocessSpendGate` is wired and what paths can still reach external models without `dispatch()`.Found parallel adapter paths — checking whether they call models outside the spend gate.**VERDICT:** The gate is an opt-in library wrapper, not a process-wide intercept—callers can still invoke model CLIs (or HTTP APIs) outside it.  
**RATIONALE:** `SubprocessSpendGate.dispatch()` only meters code that chooses to call it; nothing monkey-patches `subprocess`, blocks `os.system`/`Popen`, or enforces policy at the OS. Standing-authorized CLIs (`grok`, `gemini`) already have credentials and bill outside this module, so a one-line shell-out never hits preflight, the cost ledger, or local-first checks. A parallel in-repo path (`scripts/council/adapters.py` → `_http` / `dispatch_ex`, used by `harness.py`) also reaches providers via urllib and never imports the gate.  
**BYPASS:** `subprocess.run(["grok", "-p", <prompt>, ...])` (or `["gemini", "-p", ...]`) from any non-gated caller—e.g. any script outside `scripts/council/spend_gate.py` / `CouncilRouter.dispatch_one`.

VERDICT: The single most likely remaining way a caller could make an extern[6D[K
external model call without going through SubprocessSpendGate.dispatch() is[2D[K
is by directly using the `http` package.

RATIONALE: Although HELM has added SubprocessSpendGate to prevent bypassing[9D[K
bypassing budget/authorization controls, it's still possible for callers to[2D[K
to use the low-level `http` package to send requests directly to external m[1D[K
models. This would allow them to circumvent the control gate and make exter[5D[K
external calls without going through dispatch().
